title: Version Control with Git
subtitle: Commits, branches, merges, remotes, and how teams collaborate on code

sections.title: How Git Stores Data
sections.text:
Git is a snapshot-based version control system. Every commit is a snapshot of your entire project at that point in time. Git doesn't store changes (diffs) — it stores full snapshots but compresses identical files with references.

The three trees:

  • Working Directory — The files you're editing on disk
  • Staging Area (Index) — Files you've marked for the next commit
  • Repository (.git) — The committed snapshots

Every commit has a unique SHA-1 hash (like a1b2c3d4...) that identifies it. The commit includes the author, timestamp, message, and a pointer to its parent commit(s). This chain of commits forms the project history.
sections.caption: Git stores a chain of snapshots — each commit is a full picture of the project
sections.code:
# The three-tree workflow
$ git add file.txt          # Working → Staging
$ git commit -m "message"   # Staging → Repository
$ git status                 # See current state
$ git log --oneline          # View commit history

# Every commit is a snapshot
$ git show a1b2c3d4          # View a specific commit
$ git diff HEAD~1            # Compare with parent

sections.title: Branching & Merging
sections.text:
Branches are lightweight pointers to specific commits. Creating a branch is instant — it's just a 41-byte file with a commit hash. This makes branching cheap and encourages frequent branching.

Common branching strategies:

  • Git Flow — main (production), develop (integration), feature branches. Complex but structured.
  • GitHub Flow — Everything branches from main. Feature branch → PR → merge to main. Deploy from main. Simpler.
  • Trunk-based — Short-lived feature branches merged to main multiple times daily. Continuous integration.

Merging combines changes from one branch into another. Fast-forward merges happen when there's no divergence. Three-way merges create a merge commit when branches have diverged.
sections.caption: Feature branches isolate work-in-progress from the main codebase
sections.code:
# Branching workflow
$ git checkout -b feature/login   # create + switch
$ git add . && git commit -m "add login form"
$ git push -u origin feature/login

# Merge back to main
$ git checkout main
$ git merge feature/login
$ git branch -d feature/login     # delete local branch

# Resolve conflicts
$ git merge feature/login
# CONFLICT in file.txt → edit file.txt, then:
$ git add file.txt && git commit
sections.callout: GitHub Flow is the simplest and most popular: feature branch → PR → review → merge to main → deploy. Works for most teams.

sections.title: Working with Remotes (GitHub)
sections.text:
Remotes are copies of your repository hosted elsewhere — GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, or your own server. origin is the default name for your primary remote.

Team collaboration workflow:

  1. git clone — Copy the repository to your machine
  2. git checkout -b feature — Create a feature branch
  3. git push -u origin feature — Push your branch to GitHub
  4. Open a Pull Request (PR) — Propose your changes
  5. Team reviews, comments, requests changes
  6. Merge the PR — Changes land in main
  7. git pull — Everyone gets the latest code

Write good commit messages: short summary (50 chars), blank line, detailed body. This helps your team understand why changes were made.
sections.caption: The standard Pull Request workflow — branch, commit, push, review, merge
sections.code:
# Starting work
$ git clone git@github.com:user/repo.git
$ cd repo
$ git checkout -b my-feature

# Daily workflow
$ git add -A
$ git commit -m "Add user authentication

- Implemented JWT-based login
- Added password hashing with bcrypt
- Created middleware for protected routes"
$ git push -u origin my-feature
# Then open a PR on GitHub

# Staying updated
$ git checkout main
$ git pull origin main   # get latest
$ git checkout my-feature
$ git rebase main        # replay your changes on latest main
sections.callout: Good commit message format: subject line (50 chars max), blank line, body (72 chars per line). Explain WHAT and WHY, not HOW.
